schools.summary: The second Ziphite betrayal is set at Hachilah before Jeshimon after David's marriage to Abigail; Ellicott judges it a distinct event from the En-gedi sparing, not a doublet.
Gill: the Ziphites likely acted of themselves, fearing David's future vengeance, and sought to have him cut off by Saul's hand.
Henry: Saul, lately in a good mind, relapses when the Ziphites stir him; pray that the sparks of temptation be kept from the tinder of our corruption.

debates.question: Is this chapter a second version of the En-gedi sparing (ch. 24) or a distinct event?

sides.position: one and the same incident, differently narrated

schools.summary: The 3,000 are picked men of Saul's Gibeah standing army; Ellicott notes David wrongly supposed he would be left in peace after En-gedi.
Gill: Saul rose at once with the same disposition as ever, glad of a chance to seize David and perhaps sorry he spared him at the cave.
Saul pitches at Hachilah exactly where the Ziphites directed; Ellicott stresses that, unlike the first pursuit, David does not flee but abides and reconnoitres.
Gill: the trap is set in the very place the informers named.
K&D: David confirms the report 'for a certainty' (as at 1 Samuel 23:23) before rising with two attendants to reconnoitre.
Gill: though sure of God's protection, David uses means and keeps his guard — faith working through prudence.
The 'trench' is the wagon-rampart (Luther's wagenburg): Saul lies in the centre of a circular camp, Abner at his side, the people ringed about — an Arab-style encampment.
Gill: David came within sight to study the entrenchment with his own eye.
Wesley: but for the Ziphites, 'these wretches,' Saul would probably have pursued David no more.
Ahimelech the Hittite and Abishai (David's nephew, brother of Joab) are identified; Ellicott notes the blood feud between the sons of Zeruiah and Abner that shadows the chapter.
Henry: like Gideon, David ventures through the guards with a special assurance of divine protection; the other confidant declined.
Gill: David addressed the two men, one of whom answered the call.

themes: the sons of Zeruiah and Abner

schools.summary: Gill: David took but one companion for secrecy and was moved not by courage only but by the Spirit, assured of the God-sent sleep and seeking a second proof of his innocence.
Ellicott: the spear 'at his head' (as Jacob's stones, Genesis 28:11) is the ever-present royal emblem, reared upright beside the sleeping king.
Wesley: David acted on assurance of the kingdom, a special instinct from God, and the knowledge that God had sent the deep sleep for this second opportunity.
Clarke: Abishai repeats the very language of David's men at En-gedi (1 Samuel 24:4), and David repeats his former reply.
Ellicott: Abishai's 'not a second time' means one blow will pin Saul to the ground so as to need no other (Vulgate).
Gill: the sleeping king and the near spear together prompt Abishai's request.
Ellicott: taught by Samuel, David held Saul inviolable because the anointing made him Jehovah's possession, whose life only Jehovah may take.
Wesley: though Saul is a tyrant he is still king, and David, though designed king, is yet his subject and may neither kill him nor consent to it.
Gill: David commanded Abishai not to hurt the king.

themes: the sanctity of the Lord's anointed

schools.summary: Clarke: the three ways a man meets his day — divine stroke, natural death, or death in battle; murder would take the matter out of God's hand.
Ellicott: David names sudden death (Nabal in mind), natural death, and a battle-wound as the ways God's arm may smite Saul.
Gill: the speech is framed to quiet Abishai and prevent the blow.
Ellicott: the spear (Saul's sign of royalty) and the costly water-cruse are taken as omens of the transfer of kingship to David, in the manner of ancient royal tokens.
Gill: David speaks of touching the Lord's anointed with utmost detestation and abhorrence.
Clarke ties 'deep sleep from the Lord' to Adam's sleep (Genesis 2:21); Ellicott renders 'none saw, none knew, none awaked' and allows a natural cause — a careless, possibly carousing camp.
Gill: David took the trophies himself lest Abishai, spear in hand, be tempted to strike.
Henry: the God-sent sleep shows how easily God weakens the strongest and baffles the most watchful.

debates.question: Was the deep sleep a special miracle or natural carelessness?

sides.position: a supernatural sleep sent from the Lord upon the whole camp
not necessarily a miracle — a careless, unguarded, perhaps carousing camp

themes: the deep sleep from the Lord

schools.summary: K&D and Ellicott: David keeps a deep ravine between himself and the camp, reckoning far less on a change in Saul than he had at En-gedi (1 Samuel 24:8), and fearing capture.
Henry: David posts himself to be heard but not reached; the God-sent sleep lifts at his distant voice, a further proof of the miracle.
Wesley: 'afar off' so his person was out of reach yet his voice carried in the clear night air.
K&D and Ellicott: David calls to Abner, whose duty was to guard the king; Abner's retort means 'who disturbs the king's rest with your shouting?'
Henry: David ironically banters the negligent captain, whose easy waking confirms the God-sent sleep.
Wesley: the cry came early in the morning, at the edge of dawn.

K&D and Ellicott: David's sarcasm ('valiant man,' the noble ish) convicts Abner of a careless watch, and the missing spear proves to the listening Saul that David is his most faithful defender; a deadly feud with Abner is implied.
Clarke: 'ye are worthy to die' is strong irony; the guards' neglect deserved death, though it sprang from a supernatural sleep.
Gill: Abner, famed for valour and next under Saul, had not behaved worthily of his office.
K&D: 'ye are worthy to die' for failing to keep the Lord's anointed; the missing spear and cruse are the proof, and Saul recognizes David's voice.
Gill: 'not good' is meiosis — a grave fault stated as understatement.
Henry: 'Ecce signum' — the spear proves who is the king's true friend; those who unjustly condemn are justly left to fall into condemnation themselves.
Ellicott: the account is wholly natural — at dawn Saul hears the well-known, once-loved voice before he can discern David's figure; Gill notes Saul, now fully awake, knew it though Abner had not.
Henry: 'my son David' repeats Saul's relentings at En-gedi (1 Samuel 24:16) — a heart moved yet unchanged, glad of the voice of one whose blood he sought.
K&D: David gives well-meant counsel to seek reconciliation; Ellicott: the reverent, loving address, the royal spear glinting in David's hand, proves he sought not Saul's life.
Gill: the pursuit is beneath Saul and against his interest, for David would gladly have served him and had given no offence.
Henry: David pleads a clear conscience, backed by Saul's own word 'thou art more righteous than I' (1 Samuel 24:17).

Ellicott: the minchah offering tests whether the 'leading' comes from God; the clause reflects the OT habit of tracing all movement to God (Pharaoh, the evil spirit), yet Providence must be judged by God's law and tested by prayer.
Clarke and Wesley: if God stirred Saul, let an offering be accepted; if slanderers did, let them be cursed — the exile among idolaters says in effect 'go serve other gods.'
Henry: the deepest grief is being driven from the inheritance of the Lord; forbidding attendance on God's ordinances estranges us from God and exposes us to sin.

Ellicott reads 'far from the presence of the Lord' — David dreads dying far from Jehovah's land, the longing that made the Ark and sanctuary his life's dream; the flea and hunted partridge (with Erdmann, Conder) picture the lone, harried fugitive.
Clarke: Arabs flush and club the wearied partridge, as Saul hunted David; Wesley: God the Judge sees and will avenge the blood David will not avenge himself.
Henry: to hunt one harmless partridge with an army is a poor game unworthy of a king — 'ye have killed the just, and he doth not resist' (James 5:6).

themes: dread of dying far from God's land

schools.summary: Henry: David's second kindness melts Saul into a fuller confession, yet it springs from no principle of true repentance.
Gill: like Pharaoh, Saul's guilty conscience forced the confession, with no true sense of sin; K&D: a good intention never carried out.
Ellicott hears a ring of falseness that David sensed, declining the overtures and soon leaving Saul's realm; Clarke: 'I have sinned' may mean 'I have erred,' yet was literally true.
Wesley: nothing could be more full and ingenuous — God surely now touched his heart, and Saul attempted no more harm.

sides.position: a true touch of grace — full, ingenuous, and lasting
forced remorse without true repentance, like Pharaoh's, with a ring of falseness

themes: the last parting of Saul and David

schools.summary: Gill: the spear may have been Saul's sceptre, so David returns it lest keeping it be read as a design to seize the crown.
K&D: David bids a servant fetch back the spear and cruse, restating that he would not stretch out his hand against the Lord's anointed.
Gill: a prayer of faith — God will recompense the just and faithful, for though Saul may fail, God cannot.
K&D: not self-praise (against Thenius) but the testimony of a good conscience before God and an enemy who confesses wrong yet feels no need of forgiveness.
K&D: the deliberate parallel — as Saul's life was esteemed in David's eyes, so may David's be esteemed in Jehovah's, who will save him out of all tribulation.
Gill: David magnified Saul's life as the Lord's anointed and spared it, and asks his own be valued as highly by God.
Henry: not relying on Saul's promise, David takes God for his paymaster — a holy confidence for those who do well and suffer for it.

themes: from Saul's word to God's regard

schools.summary: Ellicott: Saul, again 'among the prophets,' unwillingly foretells David's victory ('Vicisti Nazarene'); K&D deny the blessing any genuine goodwill; the two never meet again, even in death.
Clarke: the doubled Hebrew ('in reigning thou shalt reign') and David's dignified silence refer the whole matter to God — how foolish are men's counsels against God, who saves whom none can destroy.
Henry: only a palliative cure; David dared not trust Saul's word, and they parted to meet no more — no reconciliation is firm that is not founded in peace with God through Jesus Christ.
Gill: Saul blessed David, believing he would be happy and prosperous.
